old_claim.note: Scientific Societies provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Scientific societies such as the Royal Society provided the institutional network that gathered, managed, discussed, and published scholarly correspondence.

source_shape.source_locator: The Royal Society history page says the Society gathered information by correspondence, appointed Henry Oldenburg as Secretary by 1662 to manage correspondence, held weekly meetings, and eventually published results.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source reviews the Royal Society's institutional role in managing correspondence, meetings, discussion, and publication.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The society supplies the organizational network for correspondence without being identical to the letters and printed communications themselves.

ontology_before: Scientific Correspondence Networks depended on Scientific Societies as an unsourced generic enabler.
ontology_after: Scientific Correspondence Networks keep Scientific Societies as the source-backed institutional organizer of correspondence.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Preserved: scientific_societies remains a direct enabling prerequisite with official-source evidence.
would_reject_if: The node were rescoped to private one-to-one correspondence independent of societies.
A stronger source showed the Royal Society did not manage correspondence or publish results.
A dedicated Royal Society correspondence secretary node were introduced and should replace the broader scientific_societies prerequisite.
short_reason: The Royal Society institutionalized scientific correspondence through officers, meetings, and publication.
